turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion of the Court in two cases this morning.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: The first case I have is Lindke versus Freed. Like millions of Americans, James Freed maintained a Facebook account on which he posted about a wide range of topics, including his dog, his daughter, and his job. Like many people with a Facebook account, Freed occasionally received unwelcome comments on his posts. In response, Freed took a step familiar to Facebook users. He deleted the comments and he blocked those who made them. For most people, that would have been the end of it. But Kevin Lindke, one of the unwelcomed commenters, sued Freed for violating his right to free speech. As Lindke saw it, Freed’s Facebook page was a constitutionally protected public forum from which Lindke could not be excluded based on his unwelcomed views. Because the First Amendment binds only the government, this claim is a non-starter if Freed posted on Facebook as a private citizen. But here’s the wrinkle. Freed is not only a private citizen, but also the City Manager of Port Huron, Michigan. And while Freed insists that the account is strictly personal, Lindke argues that Freed acted in his official capacity when he silenced Lindke’s speech. If Freed acted in his official capacity, the First Amendment applies. When a government official posts about job-related topics on social media, it can be difficult to tell whether the speech is official or private. That is true in Freed’s case because he posted about his work, and Lindke’s comments were critical of Freed’s work, particularly as it related to the pandemic. The Courts of Appeals have disagreed about how to analyze this kind of social media activity. The Second and Ninth Circuits focus on the appearance of the posts, treating speech as official if it comes across that way. The Sixth Circuit, by contrast, focuses on whether the official had legal authority to speak on behalf of the state. Our approach is closer to the Sixth Circuit’s. We hold that such speech is attributable to the state only if the official, first, possessed actual authority to speak on the state’s behalf, and second, purported to exercise that authority when he spoke on social media. The first prong of this test is grounded in the bedrock requirement that the conduct allegedly causing the deprivation of a federal right be fairly attributable to the state. Our President makes clear that an act is not attributable to the state unless it is traceable to the state’s power or authority. Focusing on the appearance of the posts skips this crucial step. Regardless of how official Freed’s Facebook page looks, the presence of state authority must be real, not a mirage. The authority to speak for the state can come from any of the sources listed in Section 1983, Statute, Ordinance, Regulation, Custom, or Usage. The second prong of the test we announce today acknowledges that a state official has the choice whether to speak in an official capacity or exercise his own First Amendment right to speak in his own voice. He engages in state action only if he chooses to exercise his authority to speak for the state in particular social media posts. For example, even if Freed had the authority to make official announcements about the city’s response to the pandemic, he may choose to post about the response in his capacity as a private citizen. Having authority does not mean that the official always chooses to use it. In some cases, it will be clear that an official intends to speak in a personal capacity. For example, if the official labels his account as personal, courts should conclude that posts on that account are personal unless there is significant evidence that a post constitutes the exercise of state power. Conversely, if an official makes posts on account that belongs to a political subdivision or is passed down to whomever occupies a particular office, context would make clear that those posts are official. That would be true, for example, of posts on the City of Port Huron’s official Facebook page. Freed’s page, however, lacks this context because it was not designated either personal or official. So categorizing his posts requires a fact-specific inquiry into the content and function of each relevant post. Those making official announcements that are unavailable elsewhere are clear candidates for state action, but posts that merely repeat, share, or discuss otherwise available information are more likely to be Freed’s own constitutionally protected private speech. Hard to classify cases require awareness, again, that an official does not necessarily purport to exercise his authority simply by posting about a matter within it. An official might have post job-related information for any number of personal reasons, from a desire to raise public awareness to promoting his reelection prospects. To reiterate, the State Action Doctrine requires Lindke to show that Freed first had actual authority to speak on behalf of the state on a particular matter, and second, purported to exercise that authority in the relevant posts. To the extent that this test differs from the one applied by the Sixth Circuit, we vacate its judgment and remand the case for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Our opinion is unanimous.
